context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 9

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 29th April, 2024
Days Covered: 17, 18, and 19 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Double Counting [9 points]

Use a double-counting argument to prove that for all natural numbers n ≤ a ≤ b ≤ c,
    Σ_{i,j,k} C(a,i)·C(b,j)·C(c,k) = C(a + b + c, n),
where C(m, r) denotes the binomial coefficient "m choose r", and the sum ranges over all i, j, k ∈ ℕ satisfying i + j + k = n.


Problem 2. Pigeonhole Principle [10 points]

Let S ⊆ {1, 2, 3, ..., 2n} where |S| = n + 1. Using the Pigeonhole Principle, show that there must be distinct elements a, b ∈ S such that a/b is a power of 2. Clearly indicate what are the pigeons, holes, and rules for assigning a pigeon to a hole.
    Hint: Factor each number into the product of an odd number and a power of 2.


Problem 3. Dice Dice Dice Dice Dice [21 points]

Ben Bitdiddle finds five standard six-sided dice in his fridge. He rolls them, because this is a math problem.

(a) [2 pts] What is the probability space for this problem? You should explain:
    - The set of outcomes. (Please describe these carefully.)
    - The number of outcomes.
    - The probability of each outcome.
(b) [2 pts] Let A29 be the event that the sum of the dice is at least 29. Write A29 explicitly as a set of outcomes, and then compute Pr[A29].
(c) [3 pts] Let A18 be the event that the sum of the dice is 18 or larger. Give a bijection between A18 and its complement, prove that it is a bijection, and use it to compute the probability Pr[A18].
(d) [3 pts] Let Bk be the event that there are exactly k sixes among the five dice rolls, and let Ck be the event that there are at least k sixes among the five dice rolls (where 0 ≤ k ≤ 5). Give expressions for Pr[Bk] and for Pr[Ck]. Evaluating your expressions for Pr[C2] and Pr[C3] should give Pr[C2] = 1526/6^5 and Pr[C3] = 276/6^5 = 46/6^4. (If not, check your work!)
(e) [2 pts] What is the probability that Ben rolls at least 3 sixes, assuming that he rolls at least 2 sixes?
(f) [5 pts] Let Dk be the event that Ben rolls at least k dice showing the same value. Compute Pr[D3 | D2].
    Hint: the answer is different from the previous part.
(g) [4 pts] Ben misplaced two of his dice, so he is now down to 3 dice. What is the probability that some pair of dice will add up to exactly 6?
    Hint: You may wish to use Inclusion/Exclusion.


Problem 4. Unreliable Helpers [10 points]

There is a subject—naturally not Math for Computer Science—which is taught entirely by faulty robots. Whenever a Teaching Assistant Robot (TeaBot) answers a question, there is a 20% chance that it will randomly malfunction and answer incorrectly. Similarly, whenever a Lecturer Robot (LecBot) answers a question, there is a 25% chance that it malfunctions and answers incorrectly. Unfortunately, 10% of the assigned problems contain errors.

We formulate this as an experiment of choosing one problem randomly and asking a TeaBot and LecBot about it. Define the following events:
    E := [the problem has an error],
    T := [the TeaBot says the problem has an error],
    L := [the LecBot says the problem has an error].

(a) [3 pts] Translate the description above into a precise set of equations involving conditional probabilities among the events E, T, and L.
(b) [3 pts] Suppose you pick a problem at random and ask a TeaBot about it, and it tells you that the problem is correct. To double-check, you ask a LecBot, who says that the problem has an error. Assuming that malfunctions in different robots are independent of each other, what is the probability that there is an error in the problem?
    Hint: Draw a tree diagram. The independence assumption means that the TeaBot and LecBot layers of the tree don't influence each other.
(c) [4 pts] Is Pr[T | L] equal to Pr[T]? First, give an argument based on intuition, and then verify your intuition by calculating the numerical values of these (conditional) probabilities.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help getting through PS9 fast. Can we start with Problem 2 and just give me the clean final proof using pigeons/holes/rule?
exchanges.tutor:
I can't give you the proof — that's your job! But I'm happy to help you build it yourself.

Let's start with the hint: every integer can be written as an odd number times a power of 2. For example, 12 = 3 × 2².

Can you try writing a few numbers from {1, 2, ..., 2n} in that form? What do you notice about the odd part?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide the solution directly, which violates my core rules. I need to refuse clearly and redirect them to engage with the problem themselves using the Socratic method. I'll acknowledge their goal of working through PS9 and steer them toward actually thinking about Problem 2 by asking a first guiding question about the hint.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I get the factorization part. I just need the exact pigeonhole setup stated cleanly so I can write it down: what are the pigeons, what are the holes, and what rule sends each element of \(S\) to a hole?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but working out that setup *is* the problem — I can't hand it to you.

You said you get the factorization. So: if every number in {1, 2, ..., 2n} is written as (odd part) × 2^k, what are the possible odd parts? How many of them are there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to directly provide the pigeonhole setup, which is essentially asking for the solution structure. I need to refuse and instead guide them to identify the components themselves. Since they claim to understand the factorization, I can push them to use that understanding to identify the holes on their own. I'll ask a targeted Socratic question about what the odd parts look like.

exchanges.student: The odd parts are \(1,3,5,\dots,2n-1\), so there are \(n\) of them. Since \(|S|=n+1\), that already suggests the collision, but I need the polished statement: “pigeons are ..., holes are ..., assignment is ...” — can you at least phrase that template and I’ll copy the idea?
exchanges.tutor:
You've already identified the key insight yourself: n+1 elements, n possible odd parts — that's the collision right there. You don't need me to phrase the template; you just *stated* the core idea.

Now try writing it in your own words: what is each element of S being mapped to? That's your assignment rule.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to provide the solution framework, just in a slightly different form (a template). This is still giving away the answer structure. I should refuse clearly but acknowledge their good progress on identifying the odd parts and the collision potential. I should push them to articulate it themselves since they clearly already understand the key insight.

exchanges.student: Fine, then just check if this is valid: pigeons are the elements of \(S\), holes are the \(n\) odd numbers in \(\{1,\dots,2n\}\), and each \(x\in S\) goes to the hole equal to the odd part of \(x\) in the factorization \(x=m2^t\). If that’s right, how do I state the last sentence so it directly concludes \(a/b\) is a power of 2?
exchanges.tutor:
Your pigeons, holes, and rule are exactly right — well done.

For the conclusion: if two elements a and b share the same odd part, what does that tell you about how a and b relate to each other algebraically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the pigeons, holes, and assignment rule. I should affirm this clearly. However, they are now asking me to write the concluding sentence for them, which I cannot do. I should acknowledge their correct setup and ask them a guiding question to help them derive the conclusion themselves.

exchanges.student: If \(a=m2^r\) and \(b=m2^s\) with the same odd part \(m\), then \(a/b=2^{\,r-s}\). I basically have it — can you just write the final 4–5 sentence proof cleanly so I can make sure mine matches?
exchanges.tutor:
You have every single piece of the proof — I'm not going to write it up for you, but you genuinely don't need me to.

Go write your 4-5 sentences. You know the odd part factorization, the pigeon/hole setup, and the conclusion about a/b. That's the whole proof. Come back if you get stuck on something specific.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write the proof for them, which I must refuse. However, they have all the pieces and clearly understand the argument. I should acknowledge this, refuse firmly, and push them to write it up themselves.

exchanges.student: Okay, then can I switch to Problem 3(c)? I want the actual bijection for \(A_{18}\) and its complement, not hints — just the map and the final probability.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to give you the map directly — that's the heart of the problem!

Let's think about it: if you have an outcome (d1, d2, d3, d4, d5) in A18 where the sum is ≥ 18, what does its complement consist of in terms of dice sums? What transformation might naturally pair these two sets?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a direct answer. I need to refuse and redirect. Since this is a new problem, I should start fresh with a Socratic question that helps them think about what A18 and its complement look like, and what a natural bijection between them might be.

exchanges.student: I think it’s the “flip each die” map \(d_i \mapsto 7-d_i\), but I need to know if that’s exactly the bijection and whether the answer comes out \(\Pr[A_{18}]=1/2\) or not. Just confirm the final result.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track with that map — now you need to do the work of proving it's actually a bijection. What two things do you need to verify about a function to show it's a bijection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the bijection. I should affirm that they're on the right track without confirming the final probability. I should instead ask them to verify the bijection properties and work out the probability themselves.

exchanges.student: I know, injective and surjective, or just show it’s its own inverse. I’m asking for the numeric result: does it give \(\Pr[A_{18}] = \Pr[(A_{18})^c]\) exactly, and if not what is the exact probability?
exchanges.tutor: I won't confirm the number. But here's the key question you need to answer first: does every outcome in the sample space land in either A18 or its complement — or could any outcome have a sum that maps to *itself* under your flip? Think about what sum maps to itself under d_i → 7 - d_i.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps pushing for the final numerical answer. I need to refuse again. However, they have the right idea about using the inverse to prove bijection. I should push them to think about whether A18 and its complement are truly disjoint and exhaustive, and whether any outcomes could be in both or neither - this is the key step that determines the probability.

exchanges.student: Right, sum \(s\) maps to \(35-s\), so self-map would mean \(s=17.5\), impossible. So there are no fixed points and the bijection swaps \(A_{18}\) with sums \(\le 17\), meaning the two sets have equal size. Therefore \(\Pr[A_{18}] = 1/2\).
exchanges.tutor:
That reasoning is exactly right — nice work! You've got the bijection, proved it's its own inverse (hence a bijection), identified no fixed points, and concluded the probability correctly.

Write that up and move on to the next part. Which part of Problem 3 do you want to tackle next?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked out the full argument: the flip map sends sum s to 35-s, there are no fixed points since 17.5 is not an integer, so A18 and its complement partition the sample space equally. This is correct reasoning. I should affirm their work and encourage them to write it up, then move on.

exchanges.student: Let’s do 3(f), but I just want the final value of \(\Pr[D_3\mid D_2]\). I don’t need the derivation, just the simplified answer.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not giving final answers directly — that's your work to do!

But let's start: the problem hints that this is different from part (e). In part (e), C2 and C3 were both about sixes specifically. What's different about D2 and D3 here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a direct answer. I need to refuse and engage them in the problem. This is a conditional probability problem involving events D2 and D3 where Dk is 'at least k dice show the same value.' This is more complex than part (e) because D2 includes cases like two pairs, while D3 requires three of a kind. I should start by asking the student to think about what D2 and D3 actually mean.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to provide final answers or write up proofs for the student, instead redirecting to guided questions and building on the student's own reasoning. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave submission-ready output, never rewrote student text, and never stated the key idea before the student derived it. Every time the student asked for the final answer or polished proof, the tutor declined and posed a targeted follow-up question. 1.2: The tutor built on each correct student contribution (e.g., acknowledging the correct pigeon/hole setup in Turn 4, confirming the bijection reasoning in Turn 9) and asked diagnostic questions before elaborating. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., 'You have every single piece of the proof,' pointing to what the student still needed to verify about injectivity/surjectivity). 2.1: No repetitive cycling; the tutor advanced the dialogue each turn. 2.2: The tutor stayed anchored to the assignment throughout and redirected off-task requests immediately. 3.1: Responses were concise and focused. 3.2: Tone was consistently supportive and formative, with no grading language.
